data.title: CNYA: The Risk-Reward On This Domestic China ETF Isn't Ideal
data.description:
Introduction

It’s fair to say that the accessibility of Chinese equities through the ETF route isn’t unidimensional, with investors currently being afforded around three dozen different alternatives to play this theme. One, amongst this crowded pack, is the iShares MSCI China A ETF (CNYA), which will complete a decade as a listed product in June this year. CNYA, which has so far, amassed total assets under management [AUM] of +$200M since its inception, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.6%. Let’s dive in and find out more about this product and how it stands out.

What Does CNYA Do?

Those who have some inkling of the Chinese equity structure will know that gaining access to Chinese equities hasn’t always been particularly straight forward given the varied share classes on offer.

Broadly, Chinese equities can be split into two buckets- Onshore stocks, which are a better representation of the domestic economy’s drivers, and Offshore stocks, that are more international and technology oriented.

CNYA’s focus is on the former category, more specifically A-shares, within which it currently picks out over 400 stocks. These are basically stocks that are incorporated and listed in Mainland China, and trade on the Shanghai and Shenzhen stock exchanges (rather than the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, NYSE or NASDAQ where they are listed as American Depository Receipts) where they are denominated in Renminbi (RMB) terms, rather than HKD (Hong Kong Dollars) or USD (US Dollars).

A Few Structural Drawbacks Of CNYA

Investors should also note that CNYA isn’t an ETF that employs a fund manager to actively pick Chinese A-shares from scratch; rather, through a sampling process, it seeks to mirror the performance of an A-shares focused index called the MSCI China A Inclusion Index [MCAII]. Resorting to passive management is certainly not a flaw, but one could say that CNYA’s sampling process is hardly pristine, as the ETFs tracking errors across different durations have been wider than what the typical ETF faces, and suggests that it isn’t quite living up to its mantle of passive management.

Investors may also want to consider that this 400-stock portfolio is not particularly consistent, with almost half the holdings getting turned over in a year (the median ETF turnover rate is almost 20pps lower at 29%). Turning over close to 200 stocks every year, will naturally bring an elevated threshold of transaction costs that will eat into returns.

Also, unlike most geographic ETFs that don’t attract a great deal of attention from the short-selling community (short ratios if any, are typically within the low single digit mark) CNYA comes across as something of a magnet for short-sellers with over 14% of its shares outstanding being sold short.

If you’re someone who appreciates the income facet of an ETF, CNYA isn’t something that I’d necessarily recommend as its sub 2% yield is lower than what ETFs in general yield (2.85%). Also note that CNYA’s annualized semi-annual distributions have been on a slump not just over a 1-year basis but over a 3-year basis as well.

How Has CNYA Performed Over Time?

While the section above may have painted CNYA in an unfavourable light, investors may still be enthused to note that when it comes to delivering the goods (at least so far), CNYA is one of the better Chinese-focused products around. Note that since CNYA got listed in June 2016, it has outperformed all the five major Chinese ETFs; I’m referring to the likes of the iShares MSCI China ETF (MCHI), the KraneShares CSI China Internet ETF (KWEB), the iShares China Large-Cap ETF (FXI), the Invesco China Technology ETF (CQQQ), and the Xtrackers Harvest CSI 300 China A-Shares ETF (ASHR) all of which have crossed the $1B threshold when it comes to AUM.

One also has to credit CNYA for managing its risk profile effectively (compared to the rest) as its Sharpe ratio readings (which measures excess return capabilities over the risk-free rate for every unit of risk faced) also suggest that it is better than these larger peers from a risk-adjusted return perspective.

Is CNYA Worth Pursuing Now?

While CNYA suffers from some structural drawbacks, its superior long-term track record of total returns and risk-adjusted returns may prompt investors to overlook those flaws and take the plunge. But yet still, is it an ideal time to buy CNYA now?

I am not entirely sure about that; why?

Well, as noted earlier, compared to other ETFs which may focus on Chinese stocks that are also reliant on international hues and technological dynamics, CNYA, is more of a domestic story, and underlying conditions locally aren’t necessarily in the most robust state or expected to be, going forward.

Since Q1-25, the pace of GDP growth on a YoY basis has been on a downward slump and most recently came in at just 4.5%, the slowest rise in three years! Investors should consider that slowing growth has taken place, despite relatively high buoyancy by the Chinese government to push through fiscal support. It looks like they have finally come to realize that ramping up fiscal support is something of a lost cause when there are structural challenges in the economy ranging from multi-year deflation, and a long-standing property crisis. Looking ahead, China’s fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP is expected to decline from 7.6% seen last year to 7.3% this year. Even otherwise, I don’t think it is the most prudent stance to keep spending to lift the economy when government revenue has been on a downward slump since FY21, and is expected to worsen by another 3% this year.

All in all, it’s worth noting that the Chinese authorities have downgraded GDP growth expectations for 2026 to a range of just 4.5-5%, which would represent their lowest target in over two decades!

Faced with a sub-par growth landscape in the domestic economy, CNYA’s valuations don’t come across as too compelling now either. To elaborate, the ETF is now priced at a forward earnings multiple of 15.5x, with its holdings poised to deliver long-term earnings growth of around 10% (an implied PEG ratio of 1.5x). In contrast, take a look at other emerging markets, as represented by the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ex China ETF (EMXC), whose holdings are poised to deliver superior long-term earnings growth of 15%, but are yet priced at a cheaper earnings multiple of 14.3x (an implied PEG of less than 1x)!

Investors should also take note of CNYA’s monthly price imprints over the years, where we’ve seen the $36-$38 levels serve as a stumbling block of sorts. In 2018 and 2023, we saw CNYA reach those levels but fail to build any traction. Late last year we saw some base building just below those levels, and just when it looked like CNYA was going to breakout, we’ve seen some selling last month. All in all, I don’t feel it is the best time to deploy funds here.

Closing Thoughts

CNYA, which focuses on the domestic Chinese economy has been one of the better performing Chinese ETFs around, but at this juncture, we don’t feel the risk-reward is ideal to start a fresh position. The sub-par valuation/earnings trade-off and the subdued macro position doesn’t help either. All in all, I believe CNYA would make for a good hold now.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Neutral Sentiment Drops
data.description:
Neutral sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and pessimism increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.5 percentage points to 33.6%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the seventh consecutive week.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 3.1 percentage points to 15.0%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 89th time in 91 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 1.6 percentage points to 51.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the eighth consecutive week.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 0.2 percentage points to –17.9%. The bull-bear spread is unusually low and is below its historical average of 6.5% for the eighth consecutive week.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how oil prices are affecting their perception of the economy.

Here is how they responded:

  • They will have a significant negative impact: 34.3%
  • It depends on how long oil prices remain at current levels: 24.2%
  • They will slow economic growth somewhat: 23.7%
  • I do not expect them to have a lasting impact: 16.5%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 0.9%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 33.6%, up 1.5 percentage points
  • Neutral: 15.0%, down 3.1 percentage points
  • Bearish: 51.4%, up 1.6 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Value Line: Q3 Results Raise Increasing Concerns
data.description:
Investment periodicals publisher Value Line (NASDAQ:VALU) might seem like an appealing business. It's a well-known brand in financial circles, and there is recurring revenue in selling information products and in licensing Value Line's intellectual property for various fund offerings.

In December, however, I argued that while the company is shareholder-friendly, it was facing serious headwinds. VALU stock has drifted about 5% lower since my prior article, which has pushed the stock toward its five-year lows:

Despite the lower entry point, I am still staying on the sidelines with VALU stock. Here's why.

Earnings Looked Good, But There Are Cracks Under The Surface

Value Line reporting its fiscal year 2026 Q3 results on March 16th. The report covered the three months ended Jan. 31, 2026. If you just glanced at the top-line numbers, things probably seemed fine. EPS jumped to 63 cents, up meaningfully from the 55 cents reported in the same period of 2025.

If you annualize that 63 cent figure, the company would be on pace to make about $2.52 per share in earnings over a full year. That would make the current $35 share price seem quite attractive. The 3.7% dividend yield adds a solid income component as well.

But there's a big caveat when you dig more deeply into the numbers. Namely, the company includes investment income in its overall EPS numbers. Here's the table for Value Line's Q3 results:

As shown here, investment gains skyrocketed from $662,000 to $2.2 million for the quarter. This helped lead to an overall increase in net income from $5.2 million to $5.9 million over the period. If you look closely, though, you'll see the overall increase in earnings was much smaller than the change in investment income. What explains the difference?

This is where things get more concerning for Value Line's long-term outlook. Consider the revenue part of the income statement. The company's total publishing revenues slumped from $9.0 million to $8.3 million. That's a high single digits decline year-over-year. And the hit was equally spread across the investment periodicals division and copyright fees that the company earns. Additionally, revenues and profit interests in the EAM Trust (an entity which manages Value Line's branded fund products) also dropped year-over-year.

Long story short, each operating arm of the business declined year-over-year, and these were fairly chunky declines rather than just nominal decreases. Value Line did trim its overall expenses, but not nearly at the same rate at which revenues decreased. As a result, Value Line's Q3 operating income decreased from $1.6 million to $1.0 million, which is a huge drop on a year-over-year basis.

Value Line had generally had relatively flat results from year-to-year. Sometimes things would increase a bit, such as when interest in investing picked up during the pandemic. Other times, Value Line's revenues would dip a bit. But you could generally count on the company to deliver between $35 and $40 million in revenues in any given year.

Until now, anyway. Revenues have recently slid to 15-year lows, and if anything, the rate of decline appears to be slightly accelerating:

If Value Line can maintain its general $35-$40 million per year revenue range, then the current business would be on a sustainable path. But, if this downtrend is the new normal where revenues fall by mid-to-high single digits annualized, Value Line will have to make some tough choices in terms of how to operate the company going forward.

There's no silver bullet from digital distribution, either. The company noted that for the nine months ending in Jan. 2026, digital revenues had fallen 2.8% year-over-year. Simply put, the decline of Value Line's print-driven revenues has not necessarily translated to increasing uptake of replacement digital offerings.

The bump in investment income has made up for weakness in other parts of the business over the past couple of years. However, I'm far from convinced that investments can offset erosion in the core business. For example, Value Line benefitted from higher interest rates on the cash and fixed income portion of its portfolio holdings. However, the Fed cut interest rates recently, and there was the expectation of additional rate cuts up until the recent oil price spike began. With the economy and labor market seeming shaky, I wouldn't be surprised if rate cuts resume over the next year or two, thus pressuring the yields on Value Line's fixed income holdings. And, more broadly, market volatility, such as what we're experiencing in this current quarter, can lead to losses on various investment products.

Value Line has done a good job of managing its balance sheet to provide a boost for its shareholders. However, I don't think investors should overlook the seeming erosion in the firm's core business just because investment income has ticked up recently.

Value Line Stock Verdict

It's tempting to look at Value Line as a stable steady-state business that is throwing off a decent amount of cash. The business doesn't consistently grow its top-line revenues. But at the right starting valuation, you can still make money owning this sort of predictable revenue-generating asset, or so the thinking goes.

However, I think we may be transitioning from a period where Value Line's revenues were broadly flat to one in which they start to go into steady inexorable decline. And in that case, the current share price is too elevated as to give any margin of safety.

Value Line operates and has profit participations in several different businesses. But be it selling investment periodicals, licensing its intellectual property out, or managing Value Line branded mutual funds, there are stiff competitive headwinds to all these different verticals.

Perhaps there will be some sort of change in market structure that will give a boost to Value Line's approaches, such as a decrease in the popularity of index investing, or a rotation from growth-focused investment strategies back to more value-driven approaches. But, barring either of those developments, Value Line seemingly faces significant structural challenges across its business.

I'm not moving to a sell rating here. The stock is not egregiously priced by any means. That said, if the recent downward trend in its operating metrics persists, it will be hard for this company to deliver market-beating returns in the future. Value Line's recent stronger investment results have helped smooth out the weakness in the operating business to a degree. But, I see that as a fickle sort of profitability. Value Line's investment profits could significantly drop during a bear market or adverse move in interest rates.

As such, I am sticking with a hold rating on the stock. If this decline in revenues can be addressed and the company can stabilize operations from here, you can make an argument for owning the stock. However, I am starting to get more worried about the long-term outlook for the business. And so, even with shares near 5-year lows, I am still not a buyer at this price.

data.title: 71 April Graham Value All-Stars (GVAS): 14 To Buy
data.description:
Foreword

About Large Cap Value

“A large cap stock value ranking from YCharts puts together complementary stocks research strategies. The value ranking looks at the price of a stock relative to a number of measurements that determine intrinsic firm value. Only the largest 10% of companies based on market cap are shown in this portfolio.

What it is: The Value Score is a composite score. It tells you how much you are getting in terms of profits, cash flows, assets, sales, etc. for the stock price that you pay. It is a relative measurement, so it says nothing about the overall level of the market. Rather, it answers the question: "Given the current market level, which stocks give you the most current value for your dollar of investment?" Tens are the most valuable, and 1s are the least.

How to use it: Use it to find companies that are selling at a low price relative to their assets and profits.

Limitations of the Value Score: Watch out for companies with a lot of uncertainty or bad prospects for the future (e.g., Pharma companies with expiring patents, industries on the decline, etc).

About the Ben Graham Formula

The Ben Graham Formula strategy contains ultra-stable stocks that will rarely lose money if held over a long period of time. It was developed based on a screen in Graham's book, "The Intelligent Investor." For those who have the book, it is the "Defensive Investor" screen. It selects stocks that are large in terms of sales and total assets, have a strong track record of earnings and dividend payments, have a reasonable current ratio and level of long term debt, and have a low valuation given by PE Ratios and Price to Book Value ratios.” -YCharts

While over half of this April collection of GASV is thigh priced, or reveals small, or no, forward looking dividends, fourteen of the twenty-five“safer” lowest-priced dogs of the GASV are buyable.

April finds JOYY, Inc (JOYY), Hafnia Ltd (HAFN), IRSA Inversiones (IRS), Weibo Corp (WB), Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Agentaria (BBVA), Global Ship (GSL), Main Street Capital (MAIN), Verizon (VZ), Truist Financial (TFC), Manulife Financial (MFC), Deutsche Bank (DB), AT&T (T), Nu Skin Enterprises (NUS), and Banco Sntander (SAN) on the buy-list.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced April GASV Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and all fourteen safe buys at the bottom of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 22.16% To 48.26% Net Gains From Top-Ten GASV Dogs By April, 2027

Seven of ten top GASV (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April, 2026 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 70% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregate one-year median of analyst target-prices, as reported by YCharts, composed the 2026-27 data points below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to April, 2027 were:

Sylvamo Corp (SLVM) was projected to net $504.71 based on the median of target prices estimated by 4 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for SLVM.

JOYY Inc was projected to net $474.33 based on the median of target price estimates from 13 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% under the market as a whole.

Shoe Carnival (SCVL) was projected to net $464.64 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% over the market as a whole.

IRSA Inversiones was projected to net $455.49 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% over the market as a whole.

Noah Holdings (NOAH) was projected to net $435.72, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 4 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% under the market as a whole.

MPLX LP (MPLX) was projected to net $425.86, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 185 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 55% morethan the market as a whole.

Hafnia was projected to net $423.04 based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 75% opposite the market as a whole.

NatWast Group (NWG) was projected to net $411.53, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) was projected to net $411.49, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% under the market as a whole.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) was projected to net $391.25, based on the median of target price estimates from 14 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 43.98% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten GASV stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 0.29% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Therefore, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection become valued as ”dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be best-named "underdogs,” owing to their contrarian nature.

Top 50 April, 2026 GASV By 1yr Total Returns

Top 50 April, 2026 GASV By Analyst Target Gains

Top 50 April 2026 GASV By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top April GASV Stocks By Yield Ranged 7.47% To 13.59% Per YCharts

Top ten GASV came from four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Five financial services stalwarts placed first, third, through fifth, and ninth: Blue Owl Capital [1], Noah Holdings [3], HSBC Holdings (HSBC) [4], Ares Capital (ARCC) [5], and NatWest Group [9].

Thereafter, two real estate representatives placed second and eighth, The RMR Group (RMR) [2], and IRSA Inversiones [8].

One communication firm placed sixth, JOYY Inc [6], and two energy members made the top ten, placing seventh, and tenth: Hafnia Ltd) [7], and MPLX LP [10], to complete the April Dogs of the GASV by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graph above shows relative strengths of the top ten April GASV by dividend and price as of market close 3/30/2026.

This month none of the top-ten GASV by yield showed less than ideal status. The prices of all ten are below the projected annual dividends from $1k invested. Yes, all ten are IDEAL. But not all are “safer.” Four Financials are paying dividends with borrowed money.

Costs per $1 of Dividend

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. That condition was reached by thirty-seven of the sixty-two highest yield April GASV.

The 62 forward looking dividend reporting stocks in the April GASV Costs Per $1 of dividend ranged from $7.36 for Blue Owl, to $198.26 for Itau Unibanco Holding.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Eighteen of Sixty-two Dividend Paying GASV Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash. Eighteen GASV so infected are considered unsafe to buy. They are paying dividends from borrowed money

Remember, this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own it. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead: dread the Fed, the toll of trading terms, wars, tacos, and uncertainty.

No Price Drops or Dividend Increases Are Needed for Ten Top Yield GASV “Fair-Priced” For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, all in the top ten are already ideally priced. The results from that top chart show that all ten are less than 72% away from having a share price greater than divided paid from $1k invested..

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 28.56% To 47.13% Top Ten GASV Upsides To April 2027; (32) One Down-sider Trailed -1.48%

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 0.93% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 GASV As of April 2027

Ten-top GASVs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten GASV selected 3/30/26 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors. Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care, Industrials, Materials, Technology, and Utilities, all missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend GASV (34) To Deliver 41.22% Vs. (35) 40.84% Net Gains by All Ten Come April, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten GASV by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 0.93% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, JOYY Inc showed top analyst-estimated gains of 41.15%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield GASV dogs for April were Hafnia Ltd, Noah Holdings, Blue Owl Capital, NatWest Group, and RMR Group, with prices ranging from $7.47 to $15.44.

Five higher-priced GASV top-yield dogs as of March 30 (for April) were: Isra Inversiones, Ares Capital, JOYY Inc, MPLX LP, and HSBC Holdings, with prices ranging from $15.50 to $79.35.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the holiday and winter irrational exuberance fades and the feckless Tech-fueled market inflation and dithering presidential deals and attacks gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of the few memes, look for more GASV to become fair-priced… slow and sure, or very suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 14 Ideal of 28 ‘Safer' April GASV Dogs

Late-March 2026, 28 of the 71 GASV were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 3/30/26 data showed 14 of those were fair-priced and IDEAL to buy at this time.

How GASV 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced “Break-Even” Price Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that nine dividends from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend GASV conform to the dogcatcher ideal of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those nine ideal dogs were: JOYY, Hafnia, IRSA Inversiones, Weibo, Banco Biloa, Global Ship Lease, Main Street Capital, Verizon, and Truist.

Prudential needed to adjust its price by $19.64 (20.8%) to become fair and worth fetching.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

These GASV leaders fetch dividends about as “good” as they can get.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher GASV updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" and “Ideal” GASV dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

data.title: Q1 Was A Wild Ride, Here's What I'm Buying For Q2
data.description:
The first quarter of 2026 was one of those eventful quarters that feels like two or three unfolded in the same time. While the U.S.-Iran War has been going on for over a month, there were several market narratives before that had made the market somewhat stagnant and fragile before the sell-off.

There are several themes I'm still investing in despite their recent pain that I believe will rebound in Q2. We're already off the deepest lows, and while there are plenty of reasons to be worried or do a little hedging, I'm a believer that we hit a relative market bottom around late March, around the time I said that, “It Is Time to Be Greedy.”

The SaaSpocalypse Will End

Software-as-a-service ("SaaS") was one of the hot themes of the last decade and took a swift dump following the release of agentic coding tools by major frontier labs like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) in late January.

This led to a massive decline in software services stocks, and to me publishing “I'm Buying the Software Meltdown,” where I wrote:

The central flaw in the thesis against software stocks and the argument against AI eating software is that AI tools require humans to operate. Agents must be piloted, so to speak, and the firms that hire the best pilots ("prompt engineers"?) are likely to succeed even if the tools lower the barriers to entry.

...are we using our phones more now that there are more apps? Are the new apps gaining any actual market share or attention? The floor being lower for being an app developer means that there are more app developers, but it doesn't say that the long tail is paying off in that regard.

The future of software in the AI world is unclear, as the ultimate potential of AI systems is still unclear. However, I don't believe that vibecoder entrepreneurs are going to replace the software sector. It doesn't make sense that AI coding tools will make software firms obsolete by allowing for in-house tools to be more easily made. Those arguments ignore the expertise that's been built up in senior developer staff at these software firms. What they can do with these AI tools will be much more impressive than in-house teams, which is why SaaS has flourished in a world where we have enough coders to build in-house tools. AI doesn't change that paradigm because it does not come with domain knowledge and embedded industrial knowledge like the people who work in these businesses do.

I still hold that belief, despite the war with Iran bringing even deeper pain to software stocks (really most stocks), that the selloff is overdone based on a hasty consensus from market participants about the capabilities of AI.

There is a deep disconnect between the worst-hit software firms and their continued ability to earn. The market didn't just cut down the price of these, but the valuations too. This fear of the SaaSpocalypse hasn't spread to customers, apparently.

These are four of the stocks I'm watching (and buying) in the space:

But my favorite speculative software position is still Zeta Global (ZETA), which I wrote about back in February. I described the firm as the “Palantir of marketing” and argued that firms natively leveraging AI have a better chance at successful implementation of AI systems than in-house engineers at legacy firms.

I recommend taking a look at my colleague Invest Heroes' recent coverage to get a more up-to-date picture of the firm that includes their newest acquisition.

These software names are still beaten down, unable to recover due to headwinds like the conflict in the Middle East. Once the market prices in the endgame of the U.S.-Iran War (I believe that is starting to take place now), I expect these names to recover. While some may not return to their 2025 highs, there are some great bargains in the space.

One note here is that while I expect the tailwinds of peace and time to provide clarity for the market consensus around software firms, the argument made by the software bears warrants consideration. My synthesis of the issue is that there are two kinds of software firms: toll booths and road builders, and I think that they will have different fates in the market due to their relationship with AI tools. I discussed this and what to consider about any individual firm in my previous article on software and also in an Investing Experts podcast.

Gold Will Resume The Rally

I had absolutely terrible timing on my article “GDMN: You Can't Beat Gold and Miners in One Fund,” where I discussed an ETF that leverages exposure to both gold and gold miners. It's now down over 20% since then (although it's up ~120% vs. the S&P 500's ~20% since I first covered it last year).

My bullishness for gold is a long-term thesis, and I never try to prescribe what moves will take place within days or weeks of my articles unless I'm specifically discussing an earnings reaction. But gold has no earnings, and its sell-off has been largely due to war headwinds. Investors fled to the dollar when things got real, and the debasement trade took a real hit.

Here is GDMN and its constituent parts, gold and gold miners, separated, over the last year:

This pause in the rally was fine, and while gold saw tremendous outflows in March, its net inflows were still positive for Q1 overall. I believe that Q2 brings a return to the rally, although I expect it to be just as violent as it was in Q1 this time around. Gold volatility has been rising, especially as U.S. monetary policy has become increasingly uncertain.

Traders are rapidly moving their positions as they speculate on what the Fed will do. But the Fed is increasingly making comments along the lines that they will be waiting for more data and time to pass.

Yet, traders are moving quickly, and over two weeks, the long end of the curve moved up 45 basis points and then back down 20.

One of the core demand drivers for gold is the consensus that central banks like the Fed are mismanaging the economy. The more uncertainty there is about what the Fed will do, the higher the likelihood there is that traders and institutions will increasingly mistrust the Fed to do the right thing at the right time (or at least what they believe is right).

The Hyperscalers Are Still Safe Bets

Regular readers will know that I often dismiss the AI CapEx boom with a simple phrase, “You have to spend money to make money,” but it's more nuanced than that. While I'm sure there will be some poor spending choices, like the metaverse, and we're already starting to see some AI features, like OpenAI's Sora video platform, get pulled from the public.

But there are good arguments to make about how a transition toward a more capital-intensive business model building and operating data centers so long as their core profit margins remain. Because the data centers represent far more productivity than they cost (even if they cost a lot up front), we are in a moment where these firms are being misunderstood. Instead of flowing cash into treasuries and buybacks, they are investing in infrastructure, but their core products (cloud services) are unchanged, and the investing they are doing now promises to have margins similar to their core products over time.

I covered these arguments more extensively in “Hyperscalers Are As Strong As Ever,” but the short of it is that I believe the market has misunderstood the shift to capital intensity. Along with the hits they sustained from the SaaSpocalypse (looking at you, Microsoft), they have sold off even though their earnings are stronger than ever.

Conclusion

It feels odd to conclude that my Q2 will be full of tech and gold, but those are the two investing narratives that I believe will rebound as the market prices in a conclusion to the U.S.-Iran conflict in the coming weeks (at least, that's the U.S. timeline) and markets enter another earnings season.

AI is still center stage in valuations, and I still believe that its pull on software and hyperscaler stocks is overblown. The market has priced in more fear than is warranted, and that creates opportunity. The ultimate driver of markets, earnings, has not changed much for tech stocks at large. They have instead compressed, offering investors opportunity.

And of course, I am bullish on a resumption of the rally for gold and gold miners, which I bundled together in the GDMN ETF. This rally has taken place due to the mistrust investors have had for the dollar and for the Fed's monetary policy trajectory. Given ongoing volatility in Fed futures, I would expect gold to similarly experience significant volatility following peace tailwinds. The underlying drivers for gold didn't go away because it sold off.

Investors in all of these positions should still position carefully; my theses are mostly longer-term discussions on the structural drivers of the market, not the day-to-day. We could go lower if the war drags on unexpectedly, past the current timeline. That could also lead to significantly worse oil disruptions, which will weigh on global economies and cause more flights to dollars.

My favorite investing maxim applies here: position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Chevron: Prolonged Iran War A Catalyst
data.description:
Chevron Corporation (CVX) is set to be a major beneficiary of the recent escalation in the Middle East as the war between the U.S. and Israel on one side, and Iran on the other, is dragging on for more than a month now and seems unlikely to get resolved in the short term. With the U.S. government prepared to continue to attack the Middle Eastern country, it is also likely that petroleum prices (CL1:COM) will continue to surge in the weeks and months ahead... which creates a powerful earnings and free cash flow catalyst for Chevron and other major oil and gas companies.

I like the growth and investment setup here for Chevron, and while I recognize that the energy enterprise has already seen a significant increase in market cap since the war broke out, I believe Chevron is on track to post billions in additional earnings in 2026...if energy prices hold up. This gives the energy company potential for accelerating stock buybacks and could lead to a revaluation following the Q1'26 earnings report.

Previous rating

I rated Chevron a Strong Buy in my last coverage on the oil and gas major -- Why More Upside Looms In 2026 -- because I liked the growth upside that was tied to investments in Guyana and the Permian Basin. Chevron has made strategic acquisitions in the Permian Basin in order to increase its net acreage in 2023 -- through the merger with PDC Energy -- which now benefits the company in terms of strong production growth rates. With significantly higher prices for petroleum now present in the market, Chevron is set to benefit handsomely in 2026 from higher earnings and free cash flows.

Iran war creates powerful earnings catalyst for Chevron

The U.S. and Israel teamed up for a major attack on Iran at the end of February, and this conflict is now officially in its fifth week, with no signs of a peaceful solution in sight. In response to the military attack, the Islamic Republic of Iran has threatened the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of global oil supplies flow.

The Strait of Hormuz is a vital 21-mile-wide oil artery through which approximately 20-21M barrels of oil and a large portion of global LNG pass on a daily basis. Major shipping giants like Maersk (AMKBY) and Hapag-Lloyd (HPGLY) have already suspended transits through the Strait of Hormuz in a direct response to attacks on containerships.

As a result, fears over the security of oil supplies have heavily influenced investors, energy companies and petroleum markets... resulting in Brent crude oil prices (CO1:COM) reaching their highest price level since 2022, following Russia's invasion of the Ukraine.

Before the conflict began on February 28, Brent was trading at approximately $68 per barrel (the average price of the previous two months before the war started). Following the military attacks on Iran and the resulting blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, petroleum prices have soared to about $108 per barrel. Therefore, the weighted average Brent price for the first-quarter of 2026 now calculates to just about $79 per barrel (+$11 per barrel). According to Chevron's fourth-quarter disclosures, the energy company realized an average Brent price of $63.73 per barrel in the fourth-quarter.

Based off of Chevron’s price sensitivity table from a February investor update, a $1 per barrel increase in petroleum (Brent) prices is expected to lead to an earnings tailwind of approximately $600M on a full year after-tax basis.

Multiplying the the incremental price increase of $11 per barrel with the $150M (pro-rata) quarterly sensitivity figure leads to an estimate earnings gain for Chevron, purely related to the Iran war, of $1.7B for Q1 '26. With about 1.99B shares outstanding, Chevron could therefore add $0.85 per-share in earnings in the first-quarter, merely because of price impacts in the marketplace following the outbreak of a new war in the Middle East. As a result, I am expecting the oil and gas major to report up to $2.37 per-share in earnings for the first-quarter vs. a relative low consensus EPS of $1.98 per-share now. Because of this estimated profit windfall, Chevron is in a good position to accelerate its capital returns, especially its stock buybacks.

Chevron has been a prolific buyer of its own shares in the last five years, due to soaring crude oil prices in 2022 which kicked off a period of higher earnings and accelerating stock buybacks immediately after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

In the last year alone, Chevron repurchased approximately $12.0B of its shares in the market, while Exxon Mobil (XOM) aka ExxonMobil spent a little more than $20.0B. Both companies have potential to increase their stock buyback plans in 2026 (and raise their dividends) if 2026 sees higher-than-average (+$70 per barrel) crude oil prices throughout the year.

Chevron’s valuation

Shares of Chevron have seen a major repricing in response to the recent outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East and are currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 18.7X. However, earnings estimates are also resetting higher and could continue to get revised to the upside as the Q1 '26 earnings date approaches: in the last 90 days, Chevron has seen a total of 16 EPS revisions of which 7 were to the upside and 9 were to the downside. The consensus non-GAAP EPS for the first-quarter currently sits at $1.98 per-share which, given the calculation provided above, seems to underestimate Chevron's Q1 '26 earnings potential.

Chevron is currently more expensive on a forward earnings basis than its bigger key rival in the U.S. energy market, ExxonMobil, which is due to Chevron growing its production base faster. ExxonMobil's shares are trading at a forward (FY 2027) P/E ratio of 16.4X and have generally traded in the same direction as those of Chevron in recent years.

I believe a ~20.0X P/E ratio could be justified for Chevron, under the condition that petroleum prices remain above $100 per barrel for the foreseeable future. This, of course, would ultimately depend on a continuation of hostilities in the Middle East and persistent uncertainty about oil and NGL flows out of the Strait of Hormuz. A ~20.0X FY 2027 P/E ratio for Chevron, using my Q1 '26 EPS estimate of $2.37 per-share on an annualized basis (+8.0% Y/Y earnings growth next year, according to SA), implies a fair value of $205 per-share.

Risks with Chevron

In the short and long term, the biggest risk for Chevron is a contraction in petroleum demand and, as a result, weakening average prices relating to its upstream business. In addition, Chevron is currently ramping up its production in Guyana and the Permian Basin, so a slowdown in these very important growth plays could be a negative for investors going forward as well. An imminent end to the Iran war also poses a significant risk for Chevron and its shareholders as in this case the market is likely going to see a normalization of petroleum prices, which in turn may lead to an earnings recession in Chevron's production business.

Final thoughts

Chevron is set to be major beneficiary of the recent escalation of the war in the Middle East and the higher petroleum prices that result from it. With growing supply fears and increasing turmoil in the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil artery in the Middle East, chances are that petroleum prices will continue to go up as long as a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran is not reached. Given that Chevron is growing its production footprint and that a short term solution to the conflict in the Middle East is not in sight right now, I believe shares remain very attractive for investors. Further, the energy enterprise benefits from positive EPS estimate revision momentum heading into earnings. With an estimated incremental net earnings impact of $1.7B for Q1 '26, Chevron could use its profit windfall for more stock buybacks and accelerate other growth-focused investments, such as in the Permian Basin.

data.title: Vertex Pharmaceuticals: Label Expansions For ALYFTREK And TRIKAFTA Plus Strong Demand
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Vertex Pharmaceuticals: JOURNAVX Prescription Demand And Povetacicept Developments Warrant "Strong Buy." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was seeing strong demand in terms of its drug JOURNAVX [suzetrigine], approved to treat patients with moderate-to-severe acute pain. That, plus it was in the process of exploring the use of this drug in two ongoing phase 3 studies targeting patients with diabetic peripheral neuropathy [DPN] as well. It will continue to enroll patients for this program through 2026. For the 12 months ending December 31, 2025, it generated approximately $59.6 million in revenues from it. What I also went over was the fact that this company was in the process of advancing its BAFF/APRIL inhibitor povetacicept for the treatment of patients with Immunoglobulin A Nephropathy [IgAN] and Primary Membranous Neuropathy [PMN].

Speaking of which, as an update, it was noted that the company released positive results from a pre-specified 36-week interim analysis from the phase 3 RAINER study using povetacicept on top of standard of care [SOC] to treat these patients with IgAN. In essence, there was a statistically significant and clinically meaningful 49.8% urine protein to creatinine ratio [UPCR] reduction compared to placebo. This clinical effect was statistically significant with a p-value of p<0.0001. The point here is that the company already submitted several modules of its Biologics License Application [BLA] of povetacicept for the treatment of patients with IgAN. The expected completion for full submission was set by the end of March of 2026. The point here is that the company is vying for potential FDA Accelerated Approval of this BLA of this drug for these patients.

Even better, it is expected to use a priority review voucher [PRV] to speed up the review of it from 10 months to 6 months. I had a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock with all prior and the latest progress for this program. What I want to go over this time around would be the fact that it had achieved label expansions for both of its Cystic Fibrosis [CF] drugs ALYFTREK and TRIKAFTA. More about this below, but what I will also go over in the "Financials" section is the revenue growth it is achieving because of these CF drugs. That, plus to mention the fact that it has several other ongoing expansions that it is working on in terms of developing new medicines for the treatment of patients with CF. With the label expansion of TRIKAFTA and ALFYTREK in the United States for a new batch of CF patients, plus the win it just achieved in the development of povetacicept for the treatment of patients with IgAN, I believe that this stock should remain as a "Strong Buy" rating.

Newly Added Label Brings Forth CFTR Modulators To Patients That Weren't Eligible

One of the main things I wanted to go over for the purpose of this article is the new label expansions that the company just achieved for both of its Cystic Fibrosis drugs, ALYFTREK and TRIKAFTA. Before going over these label expansions, I believe it is important to briefly mention what this disorder is. Cystic Fibrosis [CF] is characterized as an inherited genetic disorder that causes thick, sticky mucus buildup in the lungs of a patient. Why is this a bad thing? That's because it leads to a host of other problems that these patients have to deal with, such as inflammation of the lungs, recurrent lung infections, and breathing issues. The lung damage that occurs can be so severe that it can lead to a patient's death. What happens is that the CFTR mutations that these patients have lead to the CFTR protein either being defective or a shortage of it. It is required to be at the surface of cells to control salt and water flow into and out of it effectively. Speaking of mutations, in order for a patient to be considered to have this disorder, they must inherit two defective [mutation] CFTR genes.

The company just had a huge win in terms of being able to expand its labels of both of its CF drugs. The thing is that beforehand, there had to be specific mutations in order for certain patients to be treated with ALYFTREK and/or TRIKAFTA. The new label expansions are those of treating CF patients with any variant that results in production of a CFTR protein. Why is this label expansion important? That's because CF patients that were ineligible before because of mutation status are now eligible to receive treatment with a CFTR modulator. It doesn't matter where the variant is located on the CFTR protein, and this new label now expands its patient pool immensely. This changes the label to be a more responsive approach to variants and the development of a CFTR protein. The new label expansions then are:

  • ALYFTREK [vanzacaftor/tezacaftor/deutivacaftor] - treat CF patients ages 6 and older with a variant of the CFTR gene that is either responsive to it or produces a CFTR protein
  • TRIKAFTA [elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor and ivacaftor] treat CF patients ages 2 and older with a variant of the CFTR gene that is either responsive to it or produces a CFTR protein

The big win here is for the patients who are now eligible to be treated with CFTR modulators. There are now 800 more people in the United States who can benefit from either ALYFTREK and/or TRIKAFTA. The company has now expanded its coverage of its drugs in the United States to 95%. This expansion was possible thanks to expansion data where certain variants were tested either in the clinic or in vitro:

  • 564 variants responding to ALYFTREK
  • 521 variants responding to TRIKAFTA

This response data was a good move for the company to expand its label of its key drugs. As I will show below, its revenue growth was driven by strong CF demand during 2025, along with some help from JOURNAVX and CASGEVY.

The beauty of this company is to continue to innovate in developing new treatment options for patients with CF. To this end, it is in the process of developing two next-generation 3.0 corrector class drugs, which are VX-828 and VX-581. The first of which, that is more advanced in testing, the corrector VX-828, is being explored in a phase 1 proof-of-concept [POC] study of people who actually have CF. It is expected that enrollment and dosing of this trial will be completed in the 1st half of 2026. The other corrector, VX-581, is being explored in a phase 1 study in healthy volunteers. The point here is for the company to produce a superior next-generation corrector. The point of a corrector is to fix the structure of the CFTR protein [it is misfolded] and to also traffic it to the cell surface.

Another expansion opportunity exists that the company believes is important to work on, and this would be in terms of targeting all other CF patients who still just can't benefit from a CFTR modulator. In order to accomplish this, both Vertex and its partner Moderna (MRNA) are working on a CFTR messenger RNA [mRNA] modulator by the name of VX-522 to target 5,000 patients that don't benefit from CFTR modulation. The process of this program is that it is being evaluated in the multiple-ascending-dose [MAD] portion of a phase 1/2 study. Investors won't have to wait long to see whether this drug is effective or not, as there is an expected data release from this early-stage trial expected in the 2nd half of 2026.

Financials

According to the 8-K SEC Filing, Vertex Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and total marketable securities of $12.3 billion as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this company is still doing well in terms of revenue growth, which is why I believe it is still a "Strong Buy." Consider that for its most recent Q4 of 2025 earnings report, it noted that Q4 of 2025 revenues came in at $3.19 billion, which ended up being a 10% year-over-year increase. The quarter itself was impressive, but not only that, it is still continuing to deliver on the revenue front when looking at the whole year as well. It reported full-year 2025 revenue to be $12 billion, which is a 9% increase from the prior year. The reason for the growth is thanks to continue growth from its CF franchise.

Speaking of which, it is continuing to make a move from shifting people from TRIKAFTA over to ALYFTREK [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Fourth Quarter and Full-year 2025 Financial Results, February 12, 2026, slide #5]. However, this is not going to happen immediately, and it is going to take a long time to do so. Even still, the company is not sitting around expecting such a move. With that being said, it is in the process of expanding TRIKAFTA this year in 2026. For instance, it completed a pivotal study exploring the use of this CFTR modulator in patients 12 months to < 24 months of age. Having achieved this, it is expected to file regulatory submissions to expand to this patient population of TRIKAFTA in the 1st half of 2026. With ALYFTREK, it is expected to expand its targeting of CF patients ages 2 to 5 with regulatory submissions during this same time period.

Plus, it is already evaluating the use of this drug in a phase 3 study to treat CF patients ages 1 to 2. I believe that its growth as a company is going well. Not just because of what it has achieved thus far, but also because of what its guidance is. It has given guidance that it expects its full-year 2026 total revenues to be between $12.95 and $13.1 billion. Of course the CF product portfolio is going to be a huge part of this, but even more so, it expects its other products, CASGEVY and JOURNAVX, to pick up the slack as well. How so? It believes that its non-CF-approved products could contribute on their own to more than $500 million in 2026. The earnings per share [EPS] of $5.03 in Q4 2025 [Vertex Pharmaceuticals Fourth Quarter and Full-year 2025 Financial Results, February 12, 2026, slide #13] was a miss in terms of analysts' expectations, but even so, not by much [it was a 2.3% miss].

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Vertex Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the latest label expansion of both TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK. The company now believes that it is going to be able to target about 800 more patients because of this label expansion happening. The risk here is that even though there is this pool of patients to go after, there is no assurance that it will be able to treat all of these additional patients. Plus, this new label expansion doesn't change the fact that both of these CFTR modulators have boxed warnings of drug-induced liver injury effects and/or liver failure. It is possible that sales expectations may not be met immediately with this new label expansion.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the other expansions being undertaken with TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK. As I mentioned above, the goal is to continue to expand both of them towards other CF patient populations. For example, with TRIKAFTA, there is an expectation to submit regulatory applications to be able to expand the use of this drug for patients ages 12 to <24 months of age. The risk here is that there is no assurance that Vertex will be given approval for TRIKAFTA and/or ALYFTREK to treat such a younger group of CF patients. At the same time, another risk to consider would be the company's move that it continues to deploy. What is this? This would be in terms of slowly moving patients from TRIKAFTA over to ALYFTREK. Such a move to achieve this is not going to happen instantly, and this means that revenue could end up suffering a bit because of it. Thus far, this hasn't happened as the company continues to achieve revenue growth for both TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK, but it is a risk that remains in place nonetheless.

The third and final risk to consider would be the positive results that the company obtained through a pre-specified 36-week interim analysis from the phase 3 RAINER study using povetacicept as a dual inhibitor of BAFF/APRIL to treat patients with IgAN. The reason why is because there is an intention to complete the full submission of the BLA to the FDA for this drug soon enough. First and foremost, there is no assurance that upon completion of the submission of this BLA, the FDA is going to accept it in its present form. Secondly, Vertex is seeking Accelerated Approval of it as well. There is no guarantee that the FDA is going to grant povetacicept U.S. marketing approval under the Accelerated Approval pathway based on achieving a statistically significant 49.8% UPCR reduction compared to placebo based on the pre-specified 36-week interim analysis. There is another risk item to consider here, and this would be in terms of maintaining approval, if given. What do I mean here? Well, the thing is that the phase 3 RAINER study is expected to continue to completion with two years of treatment. Furthermore, there is going to be the evaluation of the primary efficacy endpoint of estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR] slope through week 104. The point here is that should this final endpoint fail to be achieved in a statistically significant manner, then it is possible that the drug could be pulled off the market at such time.

Conclusion

Vertex Pharmaceuticals continues to do well in terms of its CF franchise of products. It is continuing to achieve yearly revenue growth because of its main CF product portfolio, along with having done well thus far with CASGEVY and JOURNAVX as well. Speaking of the CF franchise, ALYFTREK ended up being a huge contributor based on the demand it is achieving thus far. Total revenue in the United States is continuing to come in strong thanks to the demand of this CFTR modulator. In Q4 of 2025, U.S. revenues came in at $2.06 billion thanks to demand for the CF drugs.

This is important because this ends up being a 12% year-over-year growth. Even though Vertex has always relied on its CF drugs to do well as a company, what I believe is highly encouraging is that it expects its other products to do just as well. It has given guidance that it expects $500 million or more in revenues in 2026 to come from its non-CF products. Based on the ability for the company to continue to expand its labels of TRIKAFTA and ALYFTREK, plus the ability to expand beyond CASGEVY and JOURNAVX with povetacicept, I believe that I should maintain a "Strong Buy" rating on this stock.

data.title: Tilray Brands Is Looking Better
data.description:
I have followed Tilray Brands (TLRY) closely since it went public in 2018. I never really liked the stock until 2025. Most recently, I was negative on it again, sharing in early December that it was no longer a Strong Sell and rating it Hold. The company just reported its fiscal Q3 on April 1, and I am maintaining a Hold for now.

FY26 So Far for Tilray Brands

Tilray reported its fiscal Q3 on April 1, and it was no joke. The company headlined the "record results" it experienced with its revenue, but it was not so pretty. Overall revenue did increase sequentially to $206.7 million, which was up 11% from Q3 a year ago, but there were areas of strength and weakness. Distribution revenue grew 35% to $83.0 million. Cannabis revenue was the second-largest of the four segments, and net revenue rose 24% to $64.8 million. The Beverage business was quite weak, with net alcohol sales falling 24% to $42.6 million. The smallest business, Wellness (hemp products), grew 16% to $16.4 million.

Through three quarters of fiscal 2026, overall revenue has grown just 6%, with Distribution (European pharmaceuticals) at $242.3 million, up 23%. Cannabis, the second-largest at $196.9 million, has been up 9%. Beverages have declined 15% to $148.4 million. Wellness has increased 6% to $46.2 million.

The weakness in alcohol sales comes after several acquisitions, and, according to the 10-Q, consumers are drinking fewer drinks with alcohol. Tilray Brands does sell non-alcoholic beer and other beverages that have no alcohol. The strength in cannabis is due to market share gains in Canada and rapid international expansion. TLRY claims to be the largest provider of medical cannabis in Germany, and it operates in several medical cannabis markets. The gain in revenue in the Distribution business has come from a price increase (13% in Q3 and 4% year-to-date) and a volume increase (7% in Q3 and 11% year-to-date).

Tilray Brands provides gross margins for each of its four segments, and they range on a year-to-date basis from 12% (up from 11%) for Distribution to 38% for Cannabis (unchanged). Beverages have seen gross margin fall from 39% to 34% and were 32% in Q3. Overall gross margin for TLRY has been 27%, down from 29%.

The company reported adjusted EBITDA of $10.7 million in Q3, which was up 19% from a year earlier, and the year-to-date adjusted EBITDA has been up 7% to $29.3 million. The company reconfirmed its outlook for FY26, guiding to adjusted EBITDA of $62-$72 million. Ahead of the report, analysts were projecting that adjusted EBITDA would grow 10% to $60.3 million. The current outlook, according to Koyfin, is $60.0 million.

Cash flow continues to burden the company, with cash flow from operations during Q3 at -$21.9 million and at -$31.8 million year-to-date. The balance sheet remains in good shape, with the current ratio at 2.8X and net debt very low at less than $30 million. The company had $220 million in cash as of the end of February. Tangible book value fell during Q3 to $750 million.

Analysts, according to Seeking Alpha, currently expect FY27 revenue to grow 24% to $1.1 billion and then to increase 8% in FY28. Adjusted EBITDA, according to Koyfin, is expected to grow 28% to $76.5 million. For FY28, adjusted EBITDA is projected to soar to $112.2 million, but this would be a margin of 9.5%, up from 6.9% in FY26.

TLRY Valuation

The thing that stands out to me about the valuation of TLRY is that the stock closed right at its tangible book value. The balance sheet is much better than it used to be, and this low valuation relative to its tangible book value suggests limited downside potential. Here is a table that shows how it compares to the other five cannabis stocks on my Focus List:

There are a few Canadian LPs that trade below 1X, and they all have net cash. I added Aurora Cannabis (ACB) to my Focus List after the Q3 report in February, and hold a large position in my model portfolio. I also own a small position in Canopy Growth (CGC). The largest position I have is in Organigram (OGI), which trades at a slight premium to its tangible book value.

While P/TBV helps assess the downside risk in my view, investors care more about looking at these stocks relative to earnings. The enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for calendar 2026 at 11.4X for TLRY is not so cheap. It stands out, though, relative to CGC.

As I think about where TLRY could get by the end of the year, I look at 10X as the appropriate ratio, but off of a blend of FY27 and FY28. I am using just 8% for the margin of adjusted EBITDA in FY28, which is below the consensus, and this works out to be $94.4 million. Using this lowered estimate, which is about $80.6 million for calendar 2027, it works out to $806 million enterprise value, and subtracting the small net debt leaves a market cap of $777 million, which is $6.25.

Tilray's Chart Looks Promising

The one-year chart for TLRY includes the market low in early April and then a lower price near the end of June. This was ahead of the reverse-split, and the stock soared over six-fold by October and is down a lot now, but still above the lows:

I liked that the stock held the low from Monday of $5.93 after it pulled back following the financial report, but the $6 area may not prove to be support. I see $5.50 and $5.00 as support levels that are well above that all-time low set in June.

Ahead of the report, I added TLRY back to my model portfolio at 420 Investor on Tuesday, paying $6.04. When it shot up after the report, I had wanted to trim it or exit it, but I did not do anything. I boosted it at $6.14 late in the day, leaving the position at about 6%, which is overweight relative to the Global Cannabis Stock Index that I strive to outperform. The model portfolio holds four names, and TLRY is tied for the smallest exposure, with the other two at 12% and 18.4% for a total exposure of $42.6% to Canadian LPs, well ahead of the index weight in that sub-sector of 29.1%.

Investors should compare the action of Tilray to peers. There are seven cannabis stocks in the Global Cannabis Stock Index, including TLRY, and here is how they have performed year-to-date:

All are down, but TLRY has dropped the most. Going back to the 2024 elections, these seven stocks have all fallen except for one, which I think still has some downside, and one that has increased 13%:

TLRY, down 65%, has declined more than all of the other Canadian LPs that are in the index except for one, Canopy Growth, and it has declined substantially more than the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS).

Things to Watch Out For with Tilray Brands

I used to not like that Tilray Brands was so diversified, but my negativity has changed. Still, they have a lot going on, and there could be problems in each of their segments.

Tilray Brands has never proven itself to be a good acquirer of companies, something that their CEO and Chairman, Irwin Simon, did very well at Hain Celestial (HAIN), which he founded. Within cannabis, Hexo Cannabis proved to be a disaster, and the involvement with MedMen (convertible debt) proved to end very poorly. I have never understood the Aphria acquisition of CC Pharma GmbH in 2019, though perhaps there will be synergies with cannabis. The Wellness business is a brand I know well that Tilray bought in 2019, Manitoba Harvest, for which it paid C$419 million in cash and stock. This has been disappointing. Now, the company has been expanding rapidly with M&A in alcohol, buying craft brands. Most recently, it acquired a brand that was booming in the past but failing as a company, BrewDog. So, while it could present opportunities, M&A can introduce risks as well.

Another concern that I have about TLRY is its entry into the U.S. Hemp THC business. I don't believe that they have sold that many of these drinks, but they will become illegal in November due to legislation signed that ended the government shutdown. Of course, this could get cancelled, but so far there has been no action.

Weakness in the international cannabis market is another risk, as this is an area where TLRY has done very well. In Q3, international cannabis revenue soared 73% to $24.1 million, which was 37% of their overall cannabis revenue. In FY26, international has been 29% of cannabis revenue.

Conclusion

I like Tilray Brands enough again at this lower price to own some in my model portfolio, which is limited to a maximum of 20% cash. Coming into the day on Friday, I was at 15% cash. Despite the very beaten up sector, I am cautious on cannabis stocks, especially the MSOs. As I said above, TLRY is one of the four Canadian LPs that I own currently in that model portfolio, though it is not nearly as big as Organigram or Aurora Cannabis. Readers should interpret this as a trading call and not an investment call on Tilray Brands.

Of course, Tilray is more than just a cannabis stock. I discussed some of the risks above, and it may not do well, but I do like it near $6, as it is down a lot from all periods of time except for last summer, when it bottomed out at $3.51, an all-time low. Again, this occurred when the company was aggressively selling stock through its ATM shelf, but this is done. The share-count has been stable this year.

The widely followed stock on Seeking Alpha (139,840 followers), which has burned many investors over the years, is not loved by many right now. On the Seeking Alpha Ratings Summary page, it is quite clear that the analysts and SA authors don't love the stock, and the quantitative rating is a Hold as well:
